_metadata.description: Rubric Quality & Typology — Auditor's Reference. 4-criterion backbone meta-rubric (Criteria Alignment, Rating Levels, Process-Oriented Assessment, Points & Weights), 4 rubric typologies (analytic / holistic / single-point / developmental), AAC&U VALUE framework, Walvoord PTA methodology, BYUI institutional context (validity & reliability), Canvas audit indicators (observational + API-grounded), rubric_quality audit-tag enum. Paired with canvas_rubrics_api_survey.md (REST surface for Rubrics, RubricAssociations, RubricAssessments + critical write-side gotchas).
_metadata.runtime_strategy_rationale: Body MD ~20,500 chars / 4 ≈ 5,125 tokens — within the 'either' band per runtime_strategy_rules. Set to read_at_runtime per named_override_patterns.selective_load: canvas_course_expert loads 2-3 of ~14 knowledge files per audit task. Selective-load defeats embed-cache benefits.
_metadata.version_state: v0.2 untested — Stage 3 revisit added Canvas API-grounded facts. Per canvas-toolbox 0.x convention, not promoted to 1.0 until a successful audit against a real Canvas course rubric. Not yet wired into canvas_course_expert.json cross_references.knowledge_files[].

scope: Reference for auditing rubrics in Canvas courses. Covers (a) the 4-criterion meta-rubric for evaluating any rubric's well-formedness, (b) the four rubric typologies — analytic, holistic, single-point, developmental — and when each is fit-for-purpose, (c) institutional and Canvas-specific audit indicators, and (d) the rubric_quality audit-tag enum. Out of scope: how to write a rubric from scratch (consuming agent's redesign playbook) and how to pull rubrics from Canvas (upcoming canvas_rubrics_api_survey + downstream tooling).

facts.definition: Audits whether each rubric criterion traces to a stated CLO, is expressed as a skill/knowledge/disposition (not a task-completion checkpoint), and whether the criteria together cover the full construct. Levels (verbatim from backbone PDF): Beginning (1) — Learning outcomes are undefined; significant gaps in the intent of what is to be measured. Developing (2) — Outcomes measurable, criteria align, but criteria not stated as skills/knowledge/dispositions. Proficient (3) — Outcomes measurable; criteria align with outcomes and are appropriate components of the construct(s). Exemplary (4) — As Proficient PLUS scope of criteria ensures the entire construct is evaluated with no gaps. Maps to BYUI validity.

Audits whether level descriptors use observable, non-subjective language, are discriminable from adjacent levels, and progress through qualitatively different performance (not just 'more' of the same). Levels (verbatim): Beginning (1) — Rating levels are subjective and/or overlap or do not align to the criteria. Developing (2) — Rating levels use subjective words; some levels progress on a continuum for some criteria, but others are disconnected. Proficient (3) — Rating levels mostly free of subjective words; differentiating between levels requires minimal inference; progression has gaps or lacks connection to levels of achievement per criterion. Exemplary (4) — Rating levels free of subjective words; progression from level to level is directly connected to distinct levels of achievement for each criterion. Flag phrases: 'shows good understanding,' 'demonstrates strong effort,' 'mostly correct,' 'somewhat thorough,' 'minor errors.' Maps to BYUI reliability. Exempted when rubric_typology = single_point.

Audits whether the rubric scores only the final artifact or also the thinking process behind it. Output-only rubrics are vulnerable in AI-rich environments. Levels (verbatim): Beginning (1) — Focus is solely on output with no attention to process. Developing (2) — Attention to evaluation of learning process is limited but present; primary focus on output product/artifact or skills required to create the output, but these are not directly aligned to assessment outcomes. Proficient (3) — Integrates evaluation of the learning process along with the output/artifact submitted. Exemplary (4) — Evaluation of both learning process and output uses the assessment plan and outcomes (not assessment instructions) as the foundation to define rubric criteria. Cross-link: evidence_centered_design_knowledge.md ai_visibility_stress_test. Exempted (default Proficient or above) when rubric_typology = developmental.

Audits whether point weights match CLO instructional emphasis, whether accountability criteria are weighted less than content criteria, and whether per-cell point ranges are eliminated in favor of discrete cell values. Levels (verbatim): Beginning (1) — Points equal across rubric aspects, even for accountability aspects; point ranges per rating level present. Developing (2) — Points distinct across aspects; accountability aspects are of lesser value than content criteria; point ranges per rating level still present. Proficient (3) — Weighting of each rubric aspect and points across rating levels align to the assessment plan and outcomes (not assessment instructions); no point ranges per rating level. Exemplary (4) — As Proficient PLUS points can be adjusted for different contexts while maintaining alignment to outcomes, with documented rationale. Anti-pattern: equal-weight rubrics across criteria of unequal CLO importance.

Breaks an assignment into discrete criteria; scores each criterion independently across performance levels (typically 3-5 levels). Multi-cell grid (criteria × levels). Most common form in Canvas. Right when criteria are separable and the assessment has multiple competencies (e.g., research paper with thesis, evidence, organization, mechanics). Strengths: targeted feedback per criterion, surfaces precise strengths/weaknesses, defensible scoring. Weaknesses: time-intensive to develop, students may not read full grid, risk of fragmenting holistic judgment into checklist mechanics.

One descriptor per overall performance level (3-5 levels). Single column; grader picks the level matching the whole work. Fit-for-purpose: standardized testing, high-volume grading where speed dominates, first-pass rough sort before analytic feedback. Strengths: fast to grade and create, produces single summative judgment. Weaknesses: no targeted feedback, students see level but not why, weak for formative use, limited learning value.

One column describing target proficiency only — no levels above or below. Optionally extended (Gonzalez 2017 variation) with two flanking columns: 'Concerns' (left, areas needing improvement) and 'Advanced' (right, areas exceeding expectations). Term coined by Mary Dietz (2000); effectiveness studied by Jarene Fluckiger (2010 — increased student achievement, especially when students help create the rubric and self-assess). Fit-for-purpose: when cost of describing every failure mode exceeds cost of personalized written feedback; when creativity matters and upper bound shouldn't be pre-described; when audience reads short rubrics but ignores long ones. Strengths: concise (students actually read it), open-ended excellence encourages creativity, quicker to create than analytic. Weaknesses: requires more detailed teacher commentary per submission. Honest tension with backbone Criterion 2 — exempted from rating_levels flag when intentionally single-point.

Pairs each criterion with process-skill descriptors of student behavior rather than performance levels. Used in scientific reasoning, oral communication, peer-process skills. Designed for student self-assessment first, instructor feedback second. Fit-for-purpose: when goal is metacognition and self-regulated learning, not summative grading. Strong fit for inquiry-based and process-oriented assessments. Strengths: improves student self-assessment accuracy over time — Czajka et al. (2021) found Lin's Concordance Correlation Coefficient r_c = 0.175 pre vs 0.403 post-feedback (students became substantially better at judging their own work). Aligns naturally with evidence_centered_design_knowledge.md process-evidence emphasis. Weaknesses: newer form, less institutional familiarity, Canvas Rubrics doesn't model it cleanly — typically implemented as analytic with process-skill criteria. Default to Proficient on backbone Criterion 3 (process-oriented by design).
citations: Czajka, Reynders, Stanford, Cole, Lantz, Ruder (2021) — J. College Sci. Teaching

facts.definition: Association of American Colleges & Universities publishes 16 VALUE (Valid Assessment of Learning in Undergraduate Education) rubrics covering LEAP outcomes — written communication, critical thinking, quantitative literacy, civic engagement, intercultural knowledge, ethical reasoning, integrative learning, others. Each VALUE rubric is analytic with 4 performance levels (Capstone, Milestones-3, Milestones-2, Benchmark). Use during audit when: (a) the course CLO maps to a LEAP outcome and the existing rubric is weak (VALUE is credible peer-reviewed starting template), (b) auditor needs benchmark for well-formed analytic rubric in a domain, (c) cross-institutional defensibility matters (accreditation, transfer credit). Limitation: VALUE rubrics are general — they intentionally don't specify discipline content; they evaluate the skill across domains.

PTA is the analytic-rubric methodology done rigorously: identify the primary traits (criteria) that define quality for the SPECIFIC assignment, then describe distinct performance levels per trait. PTA is what an Exemplary-tier analytic rubric is when criteria are DERIVED from CLOs rather than copied from a template. Key contributions: (1) Criteria derived from assignment learning goals (pre-empts Criterion 1 alignment failures). (2) Level descriptors are behavioral (what work shows) not subjective (what work seems like) — pre-empts Criterion 2 failures. (3) Departmental assessment use case: PTA rubrics roll up across course sections to support program-level assurance of learning. Cite PTA when: rubric exists but feels generic; criteria don't trace to a CLO; department needs program-level assessment evidence.

BYU-Idaho Assessment Services frames evaluation tools around two foundational criteria: Validity ('they measure what they are intended to measure' — maps to backbone Criterion 1 Criteria Alignment); Reliability ('produce consistent results over time' — maps to backbone Criterion 2 Rating Levels). Rubric Review is one of six Assessment Services offerings (alongside Course Learning Outcome Analysis, Assessment Alignment, Course Audit, Syllabus Review, Personalized Consultancy). Audit emitting rubric_quality: needs_revision with validity_flag means Criterion 1 failed; with reliability_flag means Criterion 2 failed.
citations: BYUI Campus Curriculum Development — Assessment Services (pre_knowledge/rubrics/byui_assessment_services.md)

facts.definition: (1) Overlapping or hidden criteria — two rows scoring same trait. (2) Vague descriptors — 'shows good understanding' across all levels with only adverb changes. (3) Misaligned point distribution — weights don't match CLO emphasis. (4) Static rubrics without revision — same rubric for 5 semesters without testing against student work. (5) Single-point misapplied to large anonymous-graded assessments. (6) Holistic rubric used for formative feedback (wrong typology). (7) Analytic rubric with subjective level descriptors (form is analytic but cell text is holistic-style). (8) Process-only or output-only when CLO requires both. (9) No documented rationale for weights. (10) Rubric criteria that don't trace to any CLO (orphan rubric criteria).

Flag during Canvas audit: (1) No rubric attached to a graded assignment (gap-analysis target). (2) Rubric attached but use_for_grading=false (decorative). (3) Rubric criteria don't trace to any CLO listed in syllabus or course outcomes (Criterion 1). (4) Cell text is subjective ('good,' 'fair,' 'shows understanding') without behavioral descriptors (Criterion 2). (5) Equal points across criteria for assessment whose CLOs imply unequal emphasis (Criterion 4). (6) Point ranges within a single cell ('Proficient: 6-9 points') — Criterion 4, reduces inter-grader reliability. (7) Only output-oriented criteria for assessment whose CLO targets reasoning or process (Criterion 3). (8) More than 6 or fewer than 3 criteria without stated reason (Brown range guidance). (9) Rubric used across multiple assignments with no per-assignment tailoring (generic-template signal; pre-empts Walvoord PTA). (10) Single-point rubric on 200-student auto-graded assessment (typology mismatch).
citations: This knowledge file's Canvas Audit Indicators section

facts.definition: Primary tag value enum: absent (no rubric attached to graded assignment) | needs_revision (rubric attached, at least one backbone criterion at Beginning level) | partial (rubric attached, all backbone criteria at Developing or above, at least one below Proficient) | meets_criteria (all four backbone criteria at Proficient or above). Companion tags: rubric_typology ∈ {analytic, holistic, single_point, developmental, unknown}; rubric_criteria_flags = list of backbone criteria failing (criteria_alignment, rating_levels, process_oriented, points_and_weights); rubric_use_for_grading ∈ {true, false, n/a}; validity_flag (boolean — true when Criterion 1 fails); reliability_flag (boolean — true when Criterion 2 fails). Exemption rules: when rubric_typology=single_point suppress rating_levels flag; when rubric_typology=developmental default Criterion 3 to Proficient or above.
citations: This knowledge file's Audit Tags section

facts.definition: Number of criteria (Brown Sheridan Center): 4-6 optimal — captures essential competencies without excessive grading burden. Below 4 risks losing discriminability; above 6 risks fragmentation. Number of levels (Brown): even numbers preferred (4 typical) to avoid middle-ground ambiguity where graders default to safe middle. Odd numbers (3 or 5) allow grader hedging at center; even numbers force directional judgment. Apply to backbone Criterion 2: a rubric with right number of levels can still fail Criterion 2 if descriptors are subjective or overlap (quantity is necessary, not sufficient). Single-point and developmental exempted from level count.
citations: Brown Sheridan Center — Designing Grading Rubrics

facts.definition: RubricAssociation field; when false, the rubric displays to students and graders but its total does NOT flow into the submission grade. API-grounded form of the observational 'decorative rubric' indicator. Detection: GET /api/v1/courses/:id/rubric_associations/:id, check the boolean. Tag emission: validity_flag=true; rubric_quality=needs_revision (the rubric does not measure what the grade reflects). Pair with purpose='bookmark' to disambiguate intentionally-archived from decorative.

facts.term: Canvas API Signal — criterion_use_range=true (Point Range Per Cell)
facts.definition: Rubric criterion field; when true, level cells hold a min-max range (e.g., 'Proficient: 6-9 points') instead of a discrete value. Direct API signature of the backbone Criterion 4 'point ranges per cell' anti-pattern; reduces inter-grader reliability. Detection: GET /api/v1/courses/:id/rubrics/:id, walk criteria[], check each criterion's criterion_use_range. Tag emission: rubric_criteria_flags includes 'points_and_weights'; reliability_flag=true.
citations: canvas_rubrics_api_survey.md (Nested Criteria Form — ❓ inferred, needs live-API verification)

facts.term: Canvas API Signal — used_locations.count > 1 (Multi-Association Write Hazard)
facts.definition: Not an audit failure — a write-safety warning. When a rubric is associated to multiple courses (master + sections, common in Blueprint setups), any PUT against the rubric CLONES it instead of updating in place. Source-of-truth then splits silently. Detection: GET /api/v1/courses/:id/rubrics/:id/used_locations. Any future rubric-redesign tool MUST gate writes on used_locations.count == 1; otherwise unlock-write-relock the Blueprint-side rubric (mirror of canvas_pages.upsert_page pattern). Tag emission: not a rubric_quality tag; emits as write_safety_warning on the redesign workflow.

Student-token-safe path for reading rubric criteria. Required because GET /api/v1/courses/:id/rubrics requires manage_rubrics (teacher-level) and 403s for student tokens. Returns each assignment's criteria inline but NOT the RubricAssociation metadata (use_for_grading, hide_score_total, purpose). Use this path for criterion reads when token scope is uncertain; use GET /rubric_associations when association metadata matters. Already implemented in canvas-toolbox at course_quality_check.py:588-594.
citations: AGENTS.md External System Lessons (GET /courses/:id/rubrics requires teacher token)

sources: Rubric for Evaluating a Rubric — backbone 4×4 meta-rubric (pre_knowledge/rubrics/rubrics of rubrics.pdf; author/origin to be backfilled).
Association of American Colleges & Universities (AAC&U). VALUE Rubrics (16 LEAP outcomes; pre_knowledge/rubrics/aacu_value_rubrics.pdf).
Walvoord, B.E., & Anderson, V.J. Effective Grading: A Tool for Learning and Assessment in College (Primary Trait Analysis; pre_knowledge/rubrics/walvoord_departmental_assessment.pdf).

Czajka, C.D., Reynders, G., Stanford, C., Cole, R., Lantz, J., & Ruder, S. (2021). A Novel Rubric Format Providing Effective Feedback for Learner-Centered Teaching. J. College Sci. Teaching, July/Aug 2021 (NSF #1524399, #1524936, #1524965; pre_knowledge/rubrics/nsta_novel_rubric_process_skills.md).
Gonzalez, J. (2014). Know Your Terms: Holistic, Analytic, and Single-Point Rubrics. Cult of Pedagogy.
Gonzalez, J. (2015). Meet the Single Point Rubric. Cult of Pedagogy (+ 2017 three-column variation).
Hashem, D. (2017). 6 Reasons to Try a Single-Point Rubric. Edutopia.
Sheridan Center for Teaching and Learning, Brown University. Designing Grading Rubrics.
Center for Advancing Teaching and Learning Through Research (CATLR), Northeastern University. Designing Effective Rubrics.
BYU-Idaho Campus Curriculum Development. Assessment Services (pre_knowledge/rubrics/byui_assessment_services.md).
Dietz, M. (2000) — origin of single-point rubric term.
Fluckiger, J. (2010) — single-point rubric effectiveness research.

MD body has more than 100 bytes past section headers (well exceeded)

changes: Stage 3 revisit — paired with canvas_rubrics_api_survey.md (Stage 2 output). Added 4 API-grounded facts: api_signal_use_for_grading (decorative-rubric detection), api_signal_criterion_use_range (backbone Criterion 4 API signature), api_signal_used_locations_multi (write-safety warning for redesign work), api_signal_assignment_include_workaround (student-token-safe read path already in course_quality_check.py).

Still v0.x — promotion to 1.0 gated on real-course test.
Initial authoring per make_agent_knowledge v1.0 contract. Reference shape (lookup of meta-rubric criteria, typologies, audit indicators) — parallels outcomes_quality_knowledge.md in role.
10 cited sources on disk in pre_knowledge/rubrics/ (1 backbone PDF, 2 primary PDFs, 1 full-article extraction, 5 webpage summaries, 1 institutional context).

15 facts[] entries across 4 backbone criteria (verbatim level text), 4 typologies, AAC&U VALUE, Walvoord PTA, BYUI validity/reliability, common pitfalls, Canvas audit signals, rubric_quality audit tag, number-of-criteria/levels guidance.

Single-point and developmental exemption rules documented honestly (Criterion 2 suppressed for single-point; Criterion 3 default Proficient for developmental) — backbone meta-rubric does not natively accommodate these forms.
Runtime strategy set to read_at_runtime via selective_load override (either band; selective audit-task loading pattern matches existing canvas-toolbox convention).
